abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are highly persistent environmental pollutants that pose significant risks to human and ecological health, even at ultratrace levels (ng·L⁻¹). Despite their prevalence, current detection methods rely on sophisticated laboratory equipment, precluding the possibility of real-time, on-site monitoring across distributed water infrastructures. To address this gap, we report the development of amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) designed for the selective detection of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) at parts-per-trillion concentrations. These AFPs utilize poly(p-phenylene ethynylene) and polyfluorene backbones integrated with highly fluorinated domains and pyridine-based selectors. The fluorinated regions facilitate the partitioning of PFAS into the polymer matrix, where the acidic analytes engage in a proton-transfer reaction with the pyridine units. This interaction creates low-energy exciton traps, resulting in a distinct red-shift in the fluorescence emission. We evaluated the sensing performance of these AFPs in both thin-film and nanoparticle formats, achieving detection limits of approximately 1 ppb and 100 ppt, respectively. Notably, the sensing response remained robust across various water matrices, including Milli-Q, deionized, and well water. These findings establish a versatile and sensitive platform for the rapid, field-deployable detection of aqueous PFAS at environmentally relevant scales.
